segments.text: Hey there, and welcome back to English 1314.
I am Alice, and I am so glad you are here with me today.

Whether this is your very first time visiting the channel, or you have been watching for a while, I want you to know that this is a safe, friendly space where you can grow your English one video at a time.
So, take a deep breath, get comfortable, and let us dive right in.
Now, I have a question for you.
Have you ever listened to a native English speaker and thought to yourself, "Wow, how do they do that?
How do they speak so smoothly, so naturally, without stopping to think about every single word?"
Maybe you have studied grammar for years.
Maybe you know a lot of vocabulary.
But when it comes to actually speaking, something feels a little off.
The rhythm is not quite right.
The words come out a bit too slowly.
Or maybe you understand English when you read it, but the moment someone speaks to you at normal speed, you freeze up.
If any of that sounds familiar, I want you to know you are not alone.
This is one of the most common challenges that English learners face at every level.
So, today, we are going to tackle this challenge head-on with a technique that is incredibly powerful and honestly a little bit fun.
It is called shadowing.
Now, if you have never heard of shadowing before, do not worry.
By the end of this video, you will know exactly what it is, why it works, and how to use it to seriously improve your English speaking and listening skills.
And if you have heard of it before, I promise we are going to go deeper than just the basics today.
Here is the big idea.
Shadowing is when you listen to someone speaking English and you repeat what they say almost at the same time, copying their pronunciation, their rhythm, their tone, and their natural flow.
You become like a shadow of the speaker.
You follow their voice as closely as you can.
It is used by professional interpreters, language learners, and even actors.
And the beautiful thing is, you can do it right here, right now with this video.
Today, I am going to tell you a story.
It is a real-life travel story set in an airport and on a plane.
It is perfect for practicing because it uses natural everyday English, the kind you will actually need if you travel, work, or just want to have conversations with English speakers.
The story is about a young woman named Sarah, and she is flying to New York City for the very first time.
Trust me, you are going to love it.
Here is how we are going to use the story.
I will walk you through the language in detail, section by section.
I will explain the vocabulary, the phrases, and the grammar as we go.
Then, I will give you plenty of chances to repeat after me.
And throughout the video, I will share some of my personal tips to help you get the most out of the shadowing technique.
Sound good?
Let us get started.
All right.
Let us begin with the first part of Sarah as story.
She is at the airport, and she is feeling the excitement that so many of us feel before a big trip.
Here is what she says.
I am at the airport right now, and I feel so excited.
Today is the day I am flying to New York City for the first time.
I have my ticket, my passport, and my bags.
Everything is ready.
I arrive at the check-in desk and hand my ticket to the lady.
She smiles and says, "Here is your boarding pass.
You are in seat 17A.
Have a good flight."
"Thank you," I say.
A window seat, perfect.
I love looking out of the window when I fly.
I go through security and it takes forever.
The line is so long, but I finally get through.
Now I am at gate 23 waiting to board my flight.
Okay.
Let us pause here and really dig into this opening section.
There is so much great language packed in here, and I want to make sure you understand not just the words, but how and why native speakers use them this way.
The first thing I want to look at is the phrase "Today is the day."
Sarah says.
"Today is the day I am flying to New York City for the first time."
Now, you might be wondering, "Why does she say today is the day instead of just saying today I am flying?"
Well, when a native speaker says today is the day, it carries a strong feeling of excitement, anticipation, or significance.
It means that this moment has been waited for, looked forward to, planned for.
It is a way of saying that this is not just any ordinary day.
It is the day, a special one.
You might hear people say things like, "Today is the day we move into our new house."
or "Today is the day I finally pass my driving test."
It adds emotional weight to the sentence.
Here is Alice's tip for this first section.
When you are shadowing, do not just focus on the words themselves.
Focus on the feeling behind the words.
Sarah is excited.
She is nervous.
She is full of energy.
When you repeat her sentences, try to bring that same energy into your voice.
Shadowing is not just about copying sounds.
It is about copying emotion and expression, too.
This is what makes you sound more natural and more like a native speaker.
Now, let us look at the phrase "Hand my ticket to the lady."
She says, "I arrive at the check-in desk and hand my ticket to the lady."
Notice the verb hand here.
In everyday English, we often use hand as a verb meaning to give something to someone with your hand.
So, instead of saying "I give my ticket to the lady," a more natural expression is "I hand my ticket to her."
You can use this in many situations.
For example, "Can you hand me that pen?"
Or "She handed the report to her manager."
It is a small word, but using it correctly makes your English sound much more fluid and native.
Next, let us talk about the boarding pass.
The flight attendant says, "Here is your boarding pass.
You are in seat 17A.
Have a good flight."
Now, a boarding pass is the document, or these days often a digital image on your phone, that allows you to actually get on the plane.
It is different from your ticket, which you buy in advance.
The boarding pass is what you get at the airport on the day of your flight.
It shows your seat number, your gate, and your departure time.
This is essential vocabulary if you travel.
And then we have the phrase, "It takes forever."
Sarah says, "I go through security and it takes forever.
The line is so long."
Now, takes forever is a brilliant idiomatic expression.
It does not literally mean something takes an infinite amount of time.
Of course not.
It means that something feels like it is taking a very, very long time.
Usually because it is boring, frustrating, or you are in a hurry.
You might say things like, "Oh, the traffic takes forever on Fridays."
Or, "This download is taking forever."
It is a very natural, very common phrase in casual English conversation.
Now, let us practice this section together.
I want you to repeat after me.
And remember, try to copy my rhythm and my tone as closely as you can.
I am at the airport right now, and I feel so excited.
Today is the day I am flying to New York City for the first time.
I have my ticket, my passport, and my bags.
Everything is ready.
I arrive at the check-in desk and hand my ticket to the lady.
She smiles and says, "Here is your boarding pass.
You are in seat 17A.
Have a good flight."
"Thank you," I say.
A window seat, perfect.
I love looking out of the window when I fly.
I go through security and it takes forever.
The line is so long, but I finally get through.
Now, I am at gate 23 waiting to board my flight.
Wonderful.
Keep going.
You are doing great.
Now, let us move on to the second section of the story.
This is where things start to get a little bit interesting.
After some time, I hear the announcement, "Flight 567 to New York is now boarding.
Please line up by group number."
My heart races.
This is it.
I get on the plane and walk down the aisle looking for my seat, 17A.
When I reach my row, I stop.
Someone is sitting in my seat.
It is a man in a blue jacket, and he is looking out of the window.
I feel nervous, but I say, "Excuse me, sir.
This is my seat."
He looks at me, surprised.
"Oh," he says, "are you sure?
Let me check my ticket."
He pulls out his ticket and looks at it.
"Hmm," he says, "my ticket says 17A.
I think this is my seat."
Now I am confused.
I pull out my ticket and show it to him.
"See?
My ticket says 17A," I say confidently.
He looks at my ticket, then looks at me.
He smiles kindly and says, "Okay, no problem.
You can take the seat.
I will sit in the middle."
I feel a little bad, but I really want the window seat.
"Thank you," I say as I sit down.
Okay, there is so much to unpack here.
Let us go through it carefully.
First, I want to focus on the phrase "my heart races."
Sarah says, "My heart races."
This is it.
Now, when we say someone's heart races, we mean their heart is beating very fast because they are excited, nervous, or scared.
It is a very vivid, physical way to describe a strong emotion.
You might also hear people say, "My heart was pounding."
or "My heart skipped a beat."
All of these phrases describe that intense physical feeling you get when something big is about to happen.
They are all very natural in spoken English.
And then there is the phrase, "This is it."
Just three small words, but they carry huge meaning.
When Sarah says, "This is it."
she means this is the moment.
"This is what I have been waiting for."
It is a very dramatic, emotional phrase that native speakers use all the time before a big event.
Like a musician backstage before a concert thinking, "This is it."
Or an athlete at the start line of a race.
"This is it."
Now, let us look at the interaction between Sarah and the man.
She says, "Excuse me, sir.
This is my seat."
This is a very important phrase to know.
"Excuse me."
Sir is a polite way to get someone's attention in a formal or semi-formal situation.
In English, politeness matters a lot in these kinds of awkward situations.
Notice she does not say, "Hey, that is my seat."
or "Get out of my seat."
She starts with, "Excuse me, sir."
which is respectful and non-confrontational.
This is a very good model for how to handle disagreements politely in English.
The man then says, "Okay, no problem.
You can take the seat.
I will sit in the middle."
Now, the phrase "no problem" is one of the most versatile phrases in everyday English.
It can mean you are welcome when someone thanks you.
It can mean it is fine when you want to reassure someone.
It can mean I agree when you want to show cooperation.
It is a warm, casual expression that makes the speaker sound easy-going and friendly.
Alice S.
tip for this section.
One of the best things you can do to improve your shadowing is to act out the scene as you repeat.
When you say the man's S.
lines, try to use his calm, friendly tone.
When you say Sarah's S.
lines, try to sound a little nervous, but polite.
Using different voices and emotions will help your brain remember the language much more effectively.
It also makes the practice a lot more fun.
Let us also look at the phrase, "I feel a little bad."
She says, "I feel a little bad, but I really want the window seat."
In English, when we say I feel bad, we mean we feel guilty or sorry about something.
It does not mean we are physically unwell in this context.
I feel bad about canceling our plans.
I felt bad when I forgot her birthday.
It is a very common way to express mild guilt in casual conversation.
Now, let us practice this section together.
Repeat after me.
After some time, I hear the announcement, "Flight 567 to New York is now boarding.
Please line up by group number."
My heart races.
This is it.
I get on the plane and walk down the aisle looking for my seat.
When I reach my row, I stop.
Someone is sitting in my seat.
It is a blue jacket, and he is looking out of the window.
I feel nervous, but I say, "Excuse me, sir.
This is my seat."
He looks at me surprised.
He says, "Are you sure?
Let me check my ticket.
My ticket says 17A.
I think this is my seat."
Now, I am confused.
I pull out my ticket and show it to him.
"My ticket says 17A."
I say confidently.
He smiles kindly and says, "Okay.
No problem.
You can take the seat.
I will sit in the middle."
"Thank you," I say as I sit down.
Really excellent work.
You are building your rhythm and fluency with every sentence.
Let us keep going.
Here is the third part of the story.
And this is where things get really interesting.
The plane takes off and I look out the window.
The clouds are so beautiful.
I feel happy and relaxed.
The man next to me starts a conversation.
"Are you going to New York for vacation?"
he asks.
"Yes," I say.
"It is my first time.
I want to see Times Square and the Statue of Liberty."
"That is great," he says.
"I live in New York.
You will love it.
It is a busy city, but it is full of life."
As we talk, I notice something strange.
He does not seem upset about losing the window seat.
He is calm and even friendly.
After a while, the flight attendant comes by.
She looks at me and then at the man.
"Excuse me," she says, "but I think there has been a mistake."
I freeze.
"What mistake?"
I ask.
The flight attendant looks at my ticket.
Then she looks at the seat numbers.
"You are in the wrong seat," she says.
"What?"
I ask, confused.
"The seat is 17A and it belongs to this gentleman," she explains.
"Your seat is actually 17C, the aisle seat.
My face turns red.
I look at the man and he just smiles.
"It is okay."
he says.
"I do not mind the middle seat."
I feel so embarrassed.
All this time I thought I was right, but I was wrong.
I should have been sitting in the aisle seat.
"I am so sorry."
I say to him.
"You were right all along."
"Do not worry about it."
he says with a laugh.
"Mistakes happen."
Oh, I love this part of the story.
It is full of real human emotion and brilliant everyday language.
Let us break it all down.
First, the phrase starts a conversation The man next to me starts a conversation.
This is a very useful collocation.
In English, we say start a conversation, not begin a conversation or open a conversation, although those can work in some contexts.
But in everyday casual speech, start a conversation is the most natural choice.
You might also hear the phrase strike up a conversation, which is even more informal.
He struck up a conversation with the woman at the coffee shop.
They are very similar in meaning, but start a conversation is more common at your level.
Next, look at this beautiful phrase.
It is a busy city, but it is full of life.
Full of life is a wonderful expression that means a place or a person is energetic, vibrant, exciting, and active.
You might describe a market, a neighborhood, a party, or even a person as full of life.
My grandmother is 80 years old, but she is still full of life.
The city center on a Saturday night is just full of life.
It is a very positive and expressive phrase.
Now, let us look at the phrase I freeze.
She says, The flight attendant says there has been a mistake.
I freeze.
When we say someone freezes in a social situation, we mean they suddenly become very still and cannot react or respond because they are shocked, scared, or surprised.
It is the opposite of being able to think quickly.
We also say someone is frozen to the spot or they froze up.
Have you ever been in a situation where someone asked you a question and your mind just went completely blank?
That is freezing up.
It happens to all of us.
Then we have my face turns red.
This is a very natural descriptive phrase for feeling embarrassed.
When we are embarrassed, our faces literally become red because blood rushes to the skin.
So, saying my face turns red or my face went red is a very vivid and relatable way to describe embarrassment.
You could also say, I could feel my cheeks burning, which is another great phrase for the same feeling.
And then we have the beautiful phrase, you were right all along.
All along means from the beginning, the whole time, throughout the entire situation.
So, when Sarah says, you were right all along, she is admitting that the man was correct from the very start.
She is not just saying, you were right now.
She is saying, you were right the whole time and I did not realize it.
This phrase is often used when someone finally admits they were wrong after a disagreement.
It has a slightly humble, apologetic feeling to it.
And finally, the man's as wonderful response, Do not worry about it, he says with a laugh.
Mistakes happen.
Now, do not worry about it is one of the most comforting and generous things you can say to someone in English.
It immediately takes the pressure off the other person.
It says, your mistake is not a big deal to me.
I forgive you.
Let us move on.
And mistakes happen is equally powerful.
It is a short, simple sentence, but it is so reassuring.
It reminds the other person that being wrong sometimes is just part of being human.
Alice, S tip for this section.
Pay close attention to how the man speaks in this part of the story.
He uses short, warm, generous phrases.
No problem.
It is okay.
Do not worry about it.
Mistakes happen.
These are the kinds of phrases that make a person sound truly kind and socially fluent in English.
Learning these small, powerful phrases and knowing when to use them will make a huge difference in how natural and warm you sound in English conversations.
Let us practice this section now.
Repeat after me.
The plane takes off and I look out the window.
The clouds are so beautiful.
I feel happy and relaxed.
The man next to me starts a conversation.
"Are you going to New York for vacation?"
he asks.
"Yes," I say.
"It is my first time.
I want to see Times Square and the Statue of Liberty."
"That is great," he says.
"I live in New York.
You will love it.
It is a busy city, but it is full of life."
After a while, the flight attendant comes by.
She says, "Excuse me, but I think there has been a mistake."
I freeze.
"What mistake?"
I ask.
"You are in the wrong seat," she says.
"Your seat is actually 17C, the aisle seat."
My face turns red.
I look at the man, and he just smiles.
"It is okay," he says.
"I do not mind the middle seat."
"I am so sorry," I say.
"You were right all along."
"Do not worry about it," he says with a laugh.
"Mistakes happen.
Beautiful.
Seriously, you are doing a wonderful job.
Let us continue with the final section of the story."
For the rest of the flight, I cannot stop thinking about how kind he is.
He did not argue or get upset.
He just smiled and let me have the seat.
As the plane lands, I thank him again.
"You are very kind," I say.
He just laughs and says, "Enjoy New York, and next time double-check your ticket."
When I leave the plane, I feel a mix of emotions.
Embarrassment, gratitude, and relief.
This trip is already teaching me something important.
Sometimes it is okay to admit when you are wrong.
This final section is short, but it is packed with meaning.
Let us explore the key language here.
First, "I cannot stop thinking about how kind he is.
When we say I cannot stop thinking about something, we mean it is constantly on our mind.
We keep returning to it.
We are preoccupied with it.
You might say I cannot stop thinking about that movie I watched last night.
Or she could not stop thinking about the argument she had with her friend.
It suggests that a thought is powerful enough to keep coming back to us, even when we try to focus on other things.
Then we have the phrase he did not argue or get upset.
Get upset is a very common and natural phrase for becoming emotionally distressed, angry, or troubled.
It is much more conversational than saying become angry or become emotional.
You might say try not to get upset over small things.
Or she got upset when she heard the news.
It is one of those phrases that sounds very native and very natural in everyday speech.
Next, enjoy New York.
This is a simple farewell wish.
In English, we often use the verb enjoy in this way to wish someone well before a positive experience.
Enjoy your meal.
Enjoy the show.
Enjoy the rest of your day.
It is a warm, friendly, and very natural phrase to send someone off with good energy.
And then, double-check your ticket.
The phrase double-check means to check something a second time to make sure it is correct.
When you want to be certain about something, you double-check it.
Please double-check your answer before submitting.
I need to double-check the address before we leave.
It is a very practical and widely used phrase in both professional and casual contexts.
Finally, the closing emotional reflection.
She feels a mix of emotions.
Embarrassment, gratitude, and relief.
The phrase a mix of emotions is a lovely, natural way to describe having more than one feeling at the same time, especially when those feelings are different or even a little contradictory.
After the wedding, I felt a mix of emotions, joy, sadness, and excitement.
It is much more expressive than just saying I felt many emotions.
Alice, S tip for the final section.
When you shadow the conclusion of a story, slow down a little.
The emotional, reflective parts of a story are usually spoken more slowly and thoughtfully than the action parts.
Notice how the rhythm changes.
The beginning of the story is quick and excited.
The ending is slower and more thoughtful.
Matching this rhythm is a key part of sounding natural in English.
Pace is everything.
Let us practice this final section now.
Repeat after me.
For the rest of the flight, I cannot stop thinking about how kind he is.
He did not argue or get upset.
He just smiled and let me have the seat.
As the plane lands, I thank him again.
"You are very kind," I say.
He just laughs and says, "Enjoy New York.
And next time, double-check your ticket."
When I leave the plane, I feel a mix of emotions, embarrassment, gratitude, and relief.
This trip is already teaching me something important.
Sometimes, it is okay to admit when you are wrong.
Fantastic work.
I am genuinely proud of you for making it through the full story.
Now, let us talk about something very important.
Common mistakes and nuances.
Now that we have gone through the full story, I want to take a moment to talk about some of the mistakes that English learners often make when doing shadowing.
And when using the kinds of phrases and vocabulary we practiced today.
These are small things, but they make a big difference in how natural you sound.
The first common mistake is focusing only on individual words instead of the whole phrase.
For example, many learners learn the word boarding pass separately, but the key is to learn how it fits into a sentence.
Show your boarding pass at the gate.
Have your boarding pass ready.
Can I see your boarding pass, please?
When you learn words in context, as part of real phrases, they become much easier to remember and use correctly.
The second mistake is translating too literally from your first language.
For instance, in some languages, the equivalent of I feel bad would translate more literally to I feel guilty or I feel wrong.
But in English, I feel bad is the natural, casual way to express mild guilt.
If you try to translate from your own language every time, you will often end up with something that is grammatically correct, but does not sound natural.
This is why shadowing real, authentic English is so valuable.
You are absorbing the patterns and the natural rhythms of the language directly.
The third mistake is rushing through the repetition without paying attention to intonation.
Intonation is the rise and fall of the voice.
In English, intonation is extremely important because it can completely change the meaning or the feeling of a sentence.
For example, think about how differently you can say okay, no problem.
If you say it with a warm rising tone, it sounds friendly and genuine.
If you say it with a flat monotone voice, it can sound cold or sarcastic.
When you are shadowing, please pay close attention to the intonation in my voice.
Not just the words I am saying.
The fourth mistake is giving up too soon.
This is probably the most common one.
Shadowing feels uncomfortable at first.
Your brain is doing a lot of work simultaneously.
Listening, processing, speaking, monitoring your own pronunciation.
It is genuinely hard.
But research shows that regular consistent shadowing practice, even just 10 to 15 minutes a day, produces remarkable results over time.
The discomfort you feel at the beginning is actually a sign that your brain is working hard and growing.
Embrace it.
And finally, a nuance I want you to be aware of.
In English, being polite in awkward situations is not about saying long formal sentences.
It is about using the right short phrases with the right tone.
Excuse me, sir.
No problem.
Do not worry about it.
These are brief, simple, powerful.
If you learn to use them naturally and with the right intonation, you will navigate social situations in English with real confidence and grace.
Now, let us do our interactive recap and practice.
We have covered a lot of ground today, and I want to make sure everything sticks.
Let me give you a quick summary of the key language we learned from Sarah S.
story.
From the airport scene, we learned today is the day, which expresses excitement about a significant moment.
We learned to hand something to someone, meaning to give it with your hand.
We learned boarding pass, the document you need to board a plane.
And we learned takes forever, meaning something feels like it is taking a very long time.
From the conflict on the plane, we learned my heart races, which describes a fast heartbeat from excitement or nerves.
We learned this is it, used before a big moment.
We learned excuse me, sir, as a polite way to address someone.
And we learned no problem, a versatile phrase meaning everything is fine.
From the mid-flight conversation, we learned starts a conversation or strikes up a conversation.
We learned full of life, describing a vibrant, energetic place or person.
We learned I freeze, meaning you become suddenly unable to move or react.
We learned my face turns red, a vivid description of embarrassment.
And we learned you were right all along, admitting someone was correct from the beginning.
From the conclusion, we learned I cannot stop thinking about, meaning something keeps coming back to your mind.
We learned get upset, meaning to become emotional or angry.
We learned double-check, meaning to verify something a second time.
And we learned a mix of emotions, describing having multiple different feelings simultaneously.
Now for your homework.
Yes, I said homework, but I promise it is fun homework.
Task one, read through the full script of the story on your own, out loud, without shadowing me.
Just read it clearly.
Notice which parts feel easy and which parts feel a little harder.
Those harder parts are the ones to focus on.
Task two, shadow one section of the story every day this week.
Just one section per day.
Do it three times each session.
By the end of the week, you will have practiced the whole story multiple times and it will start to feel much more natural.
Task three.
Pick three phrases from today's lesson.
Maybe no problem, takes forever, and a mix of emotions.
Try to use each one in a real conversation or in a sentence you write down in your notebook this week.
Using language in your own sentences is one of the most powerful ways to make it stick.
And task four.
This is my favorite one.
Share your practice in the comments.
Write one sentence using any phrase from today's lesson.
Something like, "The traffic on my way to work takes forever."
Or, "I felt a mix of emotions when I finished my exams."
I read every comment, and I love seeing your progress.
And there we have it.
What an incredible journey we went on together today.
From the buzzing excitement of Sarah at the check-in desk to the little mix-up on the plane to that beautiful moment of kindness and humility at the end.
Through it all, you have been building your English in a way that is real, natural, and meaningful.
If you enjoyed today's video, please give it a like.
It genuinely helps more learners like you find the channel.
And if you have not already subscribed, please do.
We have so many more great lessons coming your way, and I would love for you to be part of this community.
Share this video with a friend who is learning English, too.
The more practice, the better.
Remember, learning English is not a race.
It is a journey.
And just like Sarah on her very first flight to New York, every small step you take, every sentence you repeat, every new phrase you learn, is bringing you closer to where you want to be.
Keep going.
I believe in you.
I am Alice, and I will see you in the next lesson, right here on English 1314.
Take care.
